\section{The Witness Atom as a Cross-Domain Integer Primitive}

\subsection{Definition}

We define a \emph{Witness Atom} as the minimal admissible unit of structured observation.

A witness atom is a 4-tuple:

\[
\mathcal{W} := \langle v, N, B, h \rangle
\]

where:

\begin{itemize}
    \item $v$ is an observed value,
    \item $N$ is a declared index or address in a specified axis,
    \item $B$ is a declared basis (units, frame, normalization, transform),
    \item $h$ is a cryptographic hash binding the atom to its source artifact.
\end{itemize}

No interpretation, classification, or semantic conclusion is embedded in $\mathcal{W}$.

\subsection{Atomic Admissibility Conditions}

A 4-tuple $\mathcal{W}$ is admissible if and only if:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item (Reconstructibility) $v$ can be recomputed from the artifact slice under $B$.
    \item (Index Declaration) $N$ is explicitly declared and type-consistent.
    \item (Basis Explicitness) $B$ fully specifies units and transforms.
    \item (Integrity Binding) $h$ uniquely identifies the artifact state used to compute $v$.
\end{enumerate}

If any condition fails, the tuple is not atomic.

\subsection{Atomic Neutrality}

The witness atom carries no continuation privilege.

It does not:
\begin{itemize}
    \item authorize propagation,
    \item imply causality,
    \item imply completeness,
    \item imply truth beyond reconstructibility.
\end{itemize}

It is a constraint object, not a narrative object.

\subsection{Integer-Like Scaling Property}

Let $\{\mathcal{W}_i\}_{i=1}^n$ be a finite set of admissible witness atoms sharing a declared index axis and basis compatibility relation.

Define an aggregation operator:

\[
\mathcal{A}(\{\mathcal{W}_i\}) := \mathcal{S}
\]

where $\mathcal{S}$ is a structured set preserving:

\begin{itemize}
    \item index consistency,
    \item basis compatibility,
    \item hash lineage.
\end{itemize}

Aggregation does not modify individual atoms.
